posts.name: Scott Brunson
posts.cooked:
Which wines from the ‘61 vintage do you think are the best candidates to have life remaining?

Next year…

posts.name: Ed Gonzales
posts.cooked: la chapelle

posts.name: Christopher Foley
posts.cooked: Latour

posts.name: J. Ashourian
posts.cooked: I would say, at this point, theres significantly more risk in provenance with a wine that old than catching a particular wine that may still have fruit left. Ive had some great village level burgs from '61 from pristine provenance that certainly defy any expectation about them. Also had my share of duds. If provenance had no relevance with wine, Id say it was a great year in Champagne, Rhone, and some communes in Bordeaux. Rioja too, probably.

posts.name: Tomás Costa
posts.cooked:
If you include fortified wines, many will have probably not even reached their peak yet.

Colares reds up to the 1930s are still drinking splendidly. We have a forum user who did a vertical of them and pointed out that the 1930s didn’t feel older than the 1970s.

posts.name: Dale Williams
posts.cooked: Lots of candidates in Bordeaux and Piedmont if well stored. Palmer is legendary (and one of my top Bdx of all time), but so is pricing. Pichon Lalande is really good, and for less pricey Beychevelle and Lascombes have showed well recently.

posts.name: Martin Petersen
posts.cooked: G. Mascarello Barolo 1961, have enjoyed many fine bottles of this

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: I have a 1961 Ducru I’m excited to drink soon.

posts.name: brodie thomson
posts.cooked:
Lol, some of these suggestions make me think maybe you should have specified a price point Scott.

Scott - pricing is very important consideration. Check out the current WS pricing on 1961 Latour to give you some perspective. If this is in your price range then fine. But personally I would worry a lot about fakes with 61 Latour or 61 La Chapelle. As others have said, provenance matters a lot too

I have had perhaps 10-15 wines from 1961 in total. Good success with Red Bordeaux, Red Burg, Barolo and Sauternes. 1961 Red Bordeaux is justifiably famous as one of the greatest vintages ever. Even lesser wines can be really good. Ch Beycheville, Batailley have both been excellent. I had 1961 Latour once and it was truly amazing. Red Burgs can be good but it was not the great vintage like it was in Bdx. The 1961 red burgs I had were at Berns so that it not really that helpful for you…

Has some lovely 1961 Vallana Spannas that were not that expensive, older Barolo from Borgogno can be wonderful too. Barolo seems to a bit of a provenance minefield for older bottles so I would be very careful.

Lopez de Heredia Riojas are another good option that should be better priced than Bdx. Also these wines have often been at the winery for most of their lives so provenance is less of a concern here

The best deal out there IMO is Sauternes. I have had a couple of 1961 Sauternes including Ch Coutet. Not a great Sautenes vintage but the wines are still delicious and interesting. Also Sauternes is a much more robust wine and good bottles have great longevity.

Vintage Port is another option for you

good luck and shop early before 2021 is actually here.

Brodie

posts.name: Scott Brunson
posts.cooked:
It probably won’t be Chapelle or Latour but I’m willing to spend some bucks.
I already have some Piemonte; I’ve had lots of success with those in 61 vintage.
Re Batailley, I’ve had lots of luck with old vintages and they are priced stupid cheap.
Re Burgundy (great vintage), for my 50th I bought a 61 Gevrey Village on a visit to Trapet—it was spectacular. I was willing to go larger but they really wanted me to try it. I’m not sure we’ll get back to Burgundy before the big day (Dec of 2021) but we’ll see.
Thanks for suggestions and keep ‘em coming. I’m not sure how many times I’ll turn 60.

++1

posts.name: Anton Dotson
posts.cooked:
Wally’s has the '61 Lynch bages for 900.

The Chateau Leoville Las Cases - St.-Julien 1961 is 500-600 here and there.

Interesting: 1961 Chateau Rauzan-Segla, Margaux is 265 at Wine Consigners. That same place has 1961 Chateau Beychevelle, St. Julien for 330 bucks.

Also, stroll through Wine Searcher for 1961 and see what catches your fancy!
Well stored bottles of Haut Brion are stunning.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
As others have said, lots of 1961 Bordeaux are still in their prime, or even still a little young - provided they’ve been properly stored!

I would point to Gaffelière-Naudes as one of the right bank wines-of-the-vintage that remains surprisingly under the radar. Even if the vineyards are totally different, the style is quite similar to old Pétrus.

In the scheme of top 1961 pricing / reputation, Mouton-Rothschild is also probably comparatively underrated. The best bottles of '61 Mouton I’ve had have been better than some of the '61 Latour bottles I’ve had.

In the Médoc, hard to beat a perfect bottle of the 1961 Ducru Beaucaillou if you can find one.

My only bottle of Pichon Lalande was corked, but the underlying wine was clearly brilliant.

But 1961 was also quite an interesting year elsewhere in France.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Lots of good recommendations above.

I believe Huet Vouvrays should still be in decent shape even if it’s a somewhat austere year from the few I’ve had.

I thought Rioja was an interesting mention above. I can’t recall any 1961s although 1962 was an excellent year.

posts.name: David Griffith
posts.cooked: I recently had a 1961 Pichon Lalande that was simply amazing. The fill was not good, which added to the surprise on the upside

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
I had a '61 Nervi Gattinara last year that was very good, not great. I’ve had '61 Lanessan twice in the past 5 years (one bottle left). This is approaching great wine, and if you can find it, it will be (pardon my French) cheap!

Lopez-Heredia is a good suggestion, as is Burgundy (as long as you stay away from the trophies). I would look (to keep prices sane) for Pommard or Nuits St Georges.
About 7 years ago I had a few half-bottles of minor 1961 red Cotes de Beaune wines at Bern’s. Everything was excellent. Nothing was fading.

Dan Kravitz

posts.name: Robert Sand
posts.cooked:
61 la tache was nice last year

It need not to be la Tache - but 1961 was not bad in Burgundy either. Not as fine as in Bordeaux, nor as good as 1964 and 1962 Burgundy, but I would buy any good looking Burgundy bottle, even a Village, when the bottle condition looks excellent (and the price is ok).

PS: a 1961 Pommard Epenots or Nuits LSG is less likely a fake than La Tache 1961 !
